ingredients.name: Variable as Unknown Quantity
ingredients.description: A variable stands for a value that must be determined from the information given.

ingredients.name: Equation as Balance
ingredients.description: An equation states that two expressions have equal value and must stay balanced.

ingredients.name: Expression as Composed Operations
ingredients.description: An expression can be unpacked into operations applied in sequence to the unknown.

ingredients.name: Inverse Operations Undo Steps
ingredients.description: Addition, subtraction, multiplication, and division can be reversed to isolate the unknown.

ingredients.name: Substitution Checks a Solution
ingredients.description: A proposed solution is valid only if replacing the variable makes the equation true.

ingredients.name: Like Terms Share Structure
ingredients.description: Terms with the same variable part can be combined because they represent the same kind of quantity.

ingredients.name: Distribution Preserves Value
ingredients.description: Multiplying across parentheses rewrites an expression without changing its value.

ingredients.name: Solve Multi-Step Equations in Sequence
ingredients.description: Multi-step equations are solved by simplifying first and then undoing operations in a controlled order.

ingredients.name: Move Variable Terms While Preserving Balance
ingredients.description: When variables appear on both sides, matching changes must preserve equality while gathering terms together.

ingredients.name: Graph as All Points That Fit the Rule
ingredients.description: The graph of a linear equation is the set of points whose coordinates satisfy the relationship.

ingredients.name: Right Angle Fixes the Triangle Structure
ingredients.description: A right angle creates a special triangle with one side opposite the 90-degree angle.

ingredients.name: Legs and Hypotenuse Have Different Roles
ingredients.description: The hypotenuse is always opposite the right angle, while the legs form the right angle.

ingredients.name: Chosen Angle Determines Opposite and Adjacent
ingredients.description: Relative to a chosen acute angle, one leg is opposite and the other is adjacent.

ingredients.name: Pythagorean Relationship Links the Sides
ingredients.description: In a right triangle, the squared leg lengths add to the squared hypotenuse length.

ingredients.name: Special Right Triangles Have Fixed Ratios
ingredients.description: Certain right triangles have side lengths in predictable ratios determined by their angles.

ingredients.name: Angle as Rotation
ingredients.description: An angle measures how far a ray has rotated from a reference direction.

ingredients.name: Sine as Opposite over Hypotenuse
ingredients.description: Sine compares the opposite side length to the hypotenuse for a chosen angle in a right triangle.

ingredients.name: Cosine as Adjacent over Hypotenuse
ingredients.description: Cosine compares the adjacent side length to the hypotenuse for a chosen angle in a right triangle.

ingredients.name: Unit Circle as a Normalized Triangle
ingredients.description: A point on the unit circle creates a right triangle whose hypotenuse has length one.

ingredients.name: Sine and Cosine as Coordinates
ingredients.description: On the unit circle, cosine gives the horizontal coordinate and sine gives the vertical coordinate.

ingredients.name: Radius as a Rotating Segment
ingredients.description: A radius sweeping around a circle carries a point whose position changes with the angle.

ingredients.name: Height as Sine Projection
ingredients.description: The vertical height of a rotating point is the sine-based projection of the radius.

ingredients.name: Horizontal Position as Cosine Projection
ingredients.description: The horizontal position of a rotating point is the cosine-based projection of the radius.

ingredients.name: Period as One Full Rotation
ingredients.description: One complete turn of the radius corresponds to one full cycle of the repeating motion.

ingredients.name: Sinusoid as the Trace of Circular Projection
ingredients.description: Recording a projection value as the angle changes produces a sinusoidal graph over time or angle.

bridges.description: Seeing expressions as built from parts prepares students to group terms with matching variable structure.
Undoing a single operation directly extends to undoing multiple operations in sequence after simplification.
Understanding equality as balance directly supports moving terms from one side of an equation to the other without breaking equivalence.
Checking whether a value makes an equation true generalizes to checking whether a coordinate pair belongs on a graph.
Identifying the hypotenuse is necessary before forming the sine ratio in a right triangle.
Identifying the hypotenuse is necessary before forming the cosine ratio in a right triangle.
Knowing which side is opposite a chosen angle directly enables constructing the sine ratio.
Knowing which side is adjacent to a chosen angle directly enables constructing the cosine ratio.
The unit circle triangle relies on the same side relationship as the Pythagorean theorem once the hypotenuse is fixed to one.
Exact special-triangle ratios feed directly into common sine and cosine coordinate values on the unit circle.
Treating angle as rotation directly supports viewing a circle radius as a rotating segment.
The sine ratio becomes a vertical projection once the rotating radius forms a triangle with the axes.
The cosine ratio becomes a horizontal projection once the rotating radius forms a triangle with the axes.
Reading sine as the y-coordinate directly enables treating vertical height as a projection from circular motion.
Reading cosine as the x-coordinate directly enables treating horizontal position as a projection from circular motion.
If an angle tracks rotation, then one complete turn naturally defines one full period of the motion.
The unit-circle triangle gives the geometric setup that turns changing angle into a changing projection over time.
Once sine and cosine are understood as coordinates, plotting one coordinate against changing angle directly yields a sinusoid.
Understanding a graph as a set of valid coordinate pairs helps students reinterpret a sinusoid as the trace of projection values across inputs.

geometric.title: See expression as composed operations inside like terms share structure
geometric.body: Picture the idea of expression as composed operations as a diagram first. Then mark the exact part of that picture that becomes like terms share structure when the situation is reinterpreted in the new concept.
geometric.visual_hint: Draw a simple sketch showing expression as composed operations and highlight the feature that carries over to like terms share structure.

algebraic.title: Translate expression as composed operations into like terms share structure
algebraic.body: Write the relationship for expression as composed operations in symbols, then rewrite it in the new setting until the same structure appears as like terms share structure. Focus on what stays invariant during the translation.

card_templates.prompt: What is the exact piece of expression as composed operations that turns into like terms share structure?
